body_txt: People are individuals, and each often has their own habits, gestures, and personal idiosyncracies. Indeed, a personal gesture can be used to show that a mysterious figure hiding in shadows is a particular person by having them use a well-known gesture associated with them. However, sometimes, people pick up other people's gestures. It's commonly seen with parent/child relationships, or teacher/pupil. In fiction, it can also be used to show a sort of passing of the torch. One character has died, and they represented an ideal or were considered the Big Good , and now someone else is doing that same gesture, showing to the audience, their allies, or both (and possibly their enemies as well) that they are the successor to that person. It can also be shown when and where they pick up said gestures, often a form of behavior known as mirroring, which can be a conscious or even unconscious means of trying to relate to another person by adapting their gestures. When gestures and mannerisms are copied not out of admiration or subconsciously assumed by another person, but are instead deliberately invoked to mock the person with whom the gestures and mannerisms originate, then it is Copycat Mockery . While there can be overlap with Admiration-Based Mimicry , one need not admire or respect a person to adopt their gestures. As mentioned, it is very possible to use this for mockery, or adapting the mannerisms of a foe/opponent for the purposes of getting into their head. It can even be done to invoke a person, knowing others will recognize it and respond, even if unconsciously, even if the person adopting the gesture intensly disliked the person it was adopted from, or knew a dark and horrible secret about them. If the adopted mannerisms come from the child of a person who has died, it could overlap with Someone to Remember Him By . Especially poignant if they never got to know their late parent, but somehow still maintain the same mannerisms. Super-Trope to Irritation Is the Sincerest Form of Flattery . Sub-Trope of Character Tics , with this being someone taking on those of another character, deliberately or unconsciously. Compare Shared Family Quirks . Compare and contrast Idiosyncratic Cultural Gesture . Also compare and contrast Got Me Doing It , where someone inadvertently picks up the Verbal Tics of another character.
